title: Target Heart Rate Calculator (5 training zones)
shortDescription: Calculate your max heart rate and 5 training zones (recovery, fat burn, aerobic, threshold, max). Karvonen formula uses resting HR for accuracy.
description: Enter your age and resting heart rate. The calculator returns max heart rate (220 − age), heart rate reserve, and the 5 standard training zones. Toggle Karvonen for personalized zones based on your fitness level.
metaDescription: Free target heart rate calculator. 5 training zones (recovery, fat burn, aerobic, threshold, max) with Karvonen formula option for personalized accuracy.
empty: Enter age and resting heart rate.

z1.name: Zone 1: Recovery (50-60%)
z1.purpose: Warm-up, cool-down, rest day light activity.

z2.name: Zone 2: Fat burn (60-70%)
z2.purpose: Long endurance, fat oxidation, base building.

z5.name: Zone 5: Max (90-100%)
z5.purpose: VO₂max intervals, sprints, near maximum effort.

sections.heading: Two formulas, two levels of accuracy
paragraphs: Simple percentage of MHR: zone = max_hr × percent. Easy to compute mentally; the standard '220 − age' max-HR estimate has a ±10-12 bpm error so zones derived from it are approximate.

Karvonen formula: zone = resting_hr + (max_hr − resting_hr) × percent. Accounts for your individual resting heart rate, a proxy for fitness level. A fit person with a 50 bpm resting HR will have lower-numbered zones than an unfit person at the same age and percentage. More accurate for personalized training; the default in our calculator.

sections.heading: What each zone is for
paragraphs: Zone 1 (50-60%): Recovery. Active rest, easy walking. Heart rate returns toward baseline.
Zone 2 (60-70%): The 'fat burn' zone. Maximum reliance on fat as fuel. Long, steady-state cardio. The most popular zone for endurance athletes building a base.
Zone 3 (70-80%): Aerobic. Improves cardiovascular efficiency. Conversational pace; you can talk in short sentences but not in long ones.
Zone 4 (80-90%): Threshold. Pushing the lactate threshold. Race pace for 5K-10K runners. Hard but sustainable for 20-60 minutes.
Zone 5 (90-100%): Max effort. Used in interval training (e.g., 30 seconds hard, 90 seconds rest). Improves VO₂max and top-end speed.

sections.heading: How to actually measure heart rate
paragraphs: Wrist-based monitors (Apple Watch, Garmin, Fitbit) are accurate within ±5 bpm for steady-state cardio. Less accurate for high-intensity intervals or wrist movement (rowing, weight training).
Chest straps (Polar H10, Wahoo TICKR) are gold standard — used in research, accurate to ±1-2 bpm. Worth the investment for serious training.

Manual count: take your pulse for 15 seconds and multiply by 4. Useful for spot-checking but impractical during high intensity. Use first thing in the morning before getting out of bed for resting HR.

howTo.name: Enter your age
howTo.text: Used to estimate max heart rate (220 − age).

howTo.name: Measure resting heart rate
howTo.text: Best taken first thing in the morning while still in bed. Average over 3-7 days for accuracy.

howTo.name: Toggle Karvonen formula
howTo.text: Karvonen on (default) gives more personalized zones. Off uses simple % of max.

howTo.name: Read the 5 zones
howTo.text: Each zone shows the bpm range and its training purpose.

faq.a: It's a population average with about ±10-12 bpm individual variation. For better accuracy, do a max-HR test (run a hard 5-minute hill, last minute all-out) or get a stress test under medical supervision.

faq.q: What's a normal resting heart rate?
faq.a: Adults: 60-100 bpm. Athletes often 40-60 bpm. Below 40 may indicate excellent conditioning OR a medical condition (bradycardia) — see a doctor if you have symptoms.

faq.q: When should I be in zone 2?
faq.a: Most aerobic base training. 80% of weekly cardio time at zone 2 is a common endurance training principle (the '80/20 rule'). Maximizes fat oxidation and cardiovascular adaptations.

faq.q: Why does my watch say a different max HR?
faq.a: Modern smartwatches estimate max HR by observing your highest readings. They learn from your real efforts, so they're typically more accurate than '220 − age'. Trust your watch's number if you've done max efforts.

faq.q: Can I skip zones in training?
faq.a: You can, but balanced training has all zones in some ratio. Pure zone 5 sprints with no zone 2 base leads to plateau and burnout. Pure zone 2 with no high intensity caps your top-end fitness.

faq.q: What if my resting HR is unusually low?
faq.a: Athletes often have 40-50 bpm resting HR. Karvonen handles this correctly — your zones will be lower in absolute bpm but the same percentage of HRR. If <40 bpm and you have dizziness/fainting, see a doctor.

faq.q: Do these zones apply to swimming and cycling?
faq.a: The percentages are the same, but max HR varies by mode: cycling typically 5-10 bpm lower than running, swimming 10-15 bpm lower. Test max in each mode for accurate sport-specific zones.

faq.q: Does the data leave my browser?
faq.a: No. Calculation runs locally; nothing is sent to a server.
